name: pandas
summary: Tabular data manipulation library for Python. Standard tool for CSV/Parquet I/O, column-level transformations, groupby aggregation, and time-series resampling.

tasks.mental_trigger: I need to read from or write to CSV, Parquet, or JSON on disk
tasks.syntax:
pd.read_csv(filepath, dtype=None, usecols=None)
pd.read_parquet(filepath)
df.to_csv(path, index=False)
df.to_parquet(path, compression='snappy')
important_params: dtype — dict mapping column names to types; prevents silent object-dtype coercion
usecols — list of columns to load; skips reading unused data
compression — 'snappy' or 'gzip' for parquet; snappy is faster, gzip is smaller
index — set False on to_csv to avoid writing row numbers
tasks.example:
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', dtype={'id': str, 'score': float}, usecols=['id', 'score', 'label'])
df.to_parquet('output.parquet', compression='snappy', index=False)
tasks.use_when: Reading structured tabular files where column types need to be controlled upfront. Saving intermediate pipeline results in Parquet for faster re-reads.
tasks.avoid_when: Files exceed available RAM — use polars lazy API or dask. Need millisecond I/O — Parquet is still slower than memory-mapped formats like Arrow IPC.
tasks.decision_notes: Always prefer Parquet over CSV for any file you will read more than once — Parquet is columnar, typed, and 3-10x faster to load. Use dtype on read_csv to prevent late-stage type surprises.
gotchas: pd.read_csv infers dtypes from the first chunk only; mixed-type columns appearing later silently coerce to object dtype, breaking all downstream numeric operations.
df.to_csv writes the row index by default — always pass index=False unless you explicitly want the index preserved.

tasks.task: Inspect DataFrame structure and data quality
tasks.mental_trigger: I just loaded data and need to understand its shape, types, and null distribution before doing anything else
important_params: include='all' — include non-numeric columns in describe output
subset — list of columns for value_counts grouping
tasks.example:
df.info()                             # shape, dtypes, null counts per column
df.describe(include='all')            # stats for all columns including categoricals
df.isnull().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)  # ranked null counts
tasks.use_when: Always first step after loading any new dataset. Identifying object-dtype columns that should be numeric, spotting null patterns before imputation.
tasks.avoid_when: Production inference path — these are EDA-only operations with no place in a serving pipeline.
tasks.decision_notes: df.info() and df.isnull().sum() are the two mandatory first calls after read_csv. Skipping them is the single most common source of silent type bugs in data pipelines.
gotchas: df.describe() silently excludes non-numeric columns unless you pass include='all' — you will miss null counts in string and category columns.
df.dtypes returns a pandas Series, not a dict — equality comparison with a plain dict always returns False.

tasks.task: Select and filter rows and columns
tasks.mental_trigger: I need to extract a subset of rows by condition or columns by name
tasks.syntax:
df.loc[row_condition, col_list]
df.iloc[row_int, col_int]
df.query(expr)
df[boolean_mask]
important_params: row_condition — boolean Series or label slice for loc
col_list — list of column names to return
expr — string expression for query e.g. 'age > 30 and salary < 50000'
tasks.example:
# Label-based: rows where age > 30, only name and salary columns
subset = df.loc[df['age'] > 30, ['name', 'salary']]

# String expression (readable, avoids chaining)
active = df.query('status == "active" and score >= 0.8')
tasks.use_when: Filtering training data by label class, selecting feature columns before model input, extracting validation splits.
tasks.avoid_when: Chained bracket indexing like df[cond1][cond2] — the second filter may silently operate on a stale copy of the original DataFrame.
tasks.decision_notes: Use df.loc for combined row+column selection in one operation. Use df.query for readable multi-condition filtering. Never chain brackets for assignment.
gotchas: df.loc with integer labels on a non-default index selects by label, not position — on a reindexed DataFrame this silently picks the wrong row.
Chained assignment df[df['a']>0]['b'] = 1 triggers SettingWithCopyWarning and may not modify the original; always use df.loc for assignment.

tasks.task: Clean data: handle nulls, duplicates, types, and column names
tasks.mental_trigger: I need to fix missing values, deduplicate rows, cast column types, or rename columns before modeling
tasks.syntax:
df.dropna(subset=cols)
df.fillna(value_dict)
df.drop_duplicates(subset=cols, keep='first')
df.astype(dtype_dict)
df.rename(columns=mapping)
important_params: subset — list of columns to consider for null/duplicate checks
value_dict — per-column fill values e.g. {'age': df['age'].median()}
keep — 'first' or 'last' for which duplicate to retain
tasks.example:
df = df.dropna(subset=['label', 'text'])           # drop rows with missing target or feature
df = df.fillna({'score': df['score'].median(), 'category': 'unknown'})
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=['user_id'], keep='first')
df = df.astype({'id': str, 'score': float})
tasks.use_when: Any time raw data enters a pipeline before feature engineering or model training. Null imputation, type enforcement, and deduplication are always required.
tasks.avoid_when: Imputing without understanding the null pattern — imputing label columns destroys dataset integrity. Always verify null distributions first with isnull().sum().
tasks.decision_notes: Order matters: dropna → fillna → drop_duplicates → astype. Casting before handling nulls will fail with ValueError on NaN-containing columns.
gotchas: df.fillna(0) on an object-dtype column inserts the string '0', not the integer 0 — always cast the column to numeric first.
df.drop_duplicates without specifying subset considers all columns — valid rows differing only in a timestamp or ID will be silently retained.

tasks.task: Aggregate data with groupby, pivot, or time-series resample
tasks.mental_trigger: I need per-group statistics, cross-tabulations, or periodic summaries from a DataFrame
tasks.syntax:
df.groupby(by)[col].agg(func)
df.pivot_table(values, index, columns, aggfunc)
df.resample(rule)[col].mean()
df.merge(right, on=col, how='left')
important_params: by — column name or list for groupby key
rule — time offset for resample e.g. 'D', 'W', 'M'
tasks.example:
# Per-region sales and profit stats
result = df.groupby('region').agg({'sales': 'sum', 'profit': 'mean'})

# Monthly average — requires DatetimeIndex
monthly = df.set_index('date').resample('M')['value'].mean()

# Left join on user_id
merged = df.merge(user_df, on='user_id', how='left')
tasks.use_when: Computing class-level statistics for EDA, joining feature tables before model input, resampling sensor or transaction data to fixed time windows.
tasks.avoid_when: groupby on very high-cardinality string keys — consider integer encoding first. resample on non-datetime index — it will raise TypeError with a confusing message.
tasks.decision_notes: For groupby with categoricals, always pass observed=True to avoid phantom groups with NaN aggregates. For merges, always check the row count after merge to detect accidental fan-out from duplicate keys.
gotchas: df.groupby on a categorical column returns NaN aggregates for all categories not present in the data unless observed=True is set.
df.resample requires a DatetimeIndex — calling it on a string or integer index raises TypeError without telling you to set the index first.
